data.title: Ambipar Emergency Response Falls to 52-Week Low, Resumes Trading After Bankruptcy
data.description:
By Josh Beckerman

Ambipar Emergency Response shares declined 33% to 75 cents on Thursday, resuming trading after being halted since Tuesday following the company's bankruptcy filing.

Intraday prices ranged from 48 cents, which marked a 52-week low, to 95 cents.

Ambipar filed for chapter 11 bankruptcy in Texas on Monday, while parent company Ambipar Participações e Empreendimentos filed for insolvency protection in Brazil. The company said Tuesday morning that normal operations are continuing.

Ambipar Emergency Response provides services including crisis management and responding to environmental, chemical and biological emergencies.

NYSE American said Tuesday that it would commence delisting proceedings. The company has the right to appeal.

Write to Josh Beckerman at josh.beckerman@wsj.com

data.title: Vinci 9-Month Revenues Up 3.7%
data.description:
RUEIL-MALMAISON (dpa-AFX) - Vinci SA (VCISY.PK) Thursday reported its nine-month revenues of 54.25 billion euros, up 3.7% from 52.31 billion euros last year. On a like-for-like basis, revenues rose 2.0%.

Concession revenues gained 5.4% to 9.38 billion euros, while Energy Solutions gained 6.7% to 20.73 billion euros. Construction gained 0.8% to 24.48 billion euros.

Outside France, revenue came to 31.36 billion euros, up 5.3% on an actual basis and up 2.3% on a like-for-like basis.

In France, revenue was 22.90 billion euros, up 1.6% on an actual basis and up 1.6% on a like-for-like basis.

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: CACI INTERNATIONAL INC /DE/ SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
CACI International Inc., a leading provider of expertise and technology services to the U.S. government, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter. The report highlights significant financial growth and operational achievements, driven by increased demand for the company's services and strategic contract awards.

Financial Highlights

Revenues: $2,287.6 million, reflecting an increase of 11.2% compared to the same period last year, driven by organic growth and new contract awards.

Income from operations: $212.3 million, representing an 18.0% increase from the prior year period, indicating improved operational efficiency.

Net income: $124.8 million, up 3.9% from the previous year, supported by higher revenues and operational improvements.

Basic earnings per share: $5.67, compared to $5.39 in the prior year, reflecting the company's profitability growth.

Diluted earnings per share: $5.63, up from $5.33 in the previous year, highlighting increased earnings per share due to improved net income.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company reported revenue growth across all customer segments, with the Department of Defense (DoD) contributing $1,179,626,000, the Intelligence Community (IC) $596,429,000, Federal civilian agencies $411,730,000, and Commercial and other sectors $99,838,000. This reflects an increase in demand for both Expertise and Technology services.

Geographical Performance: Domestic operations generated $2,210,043,000 in revenue, while international operations contributed $77,580,000. The domestic market remains the primary revenue driver, with significant contributions from defense-related customers.

Operational Efficiency: The company achieved cost efficiencies, as indicated by a slight decrease in indirect costs and selling expenses as a percentage of revenue, from 20.8% to 20.7%, despite an increase in absolute terms due to higher labor costs.

Future Outlook: The company anticipates continued growth in its addressable market, driven by stable-to-higher U.S. government budget environments, particularly in national security-related areas. There is an expected increase in demand for advanced technologies, including AI and network modernization, which aligns with the company's strategic focus.

Contract Backlog: The total contract backlog increased to $33.9 billion, with a funded backlog of $5.4 billion, indicating a strong pipeline of future work. This backlog includes both funded and unfunded contracts, reflecting the company's robust position in securing long-term projects.

SEC Filing: CACI INTERNATIONAL INC /DE/ [ CACI ] - 10-Q - Oct. 23, 2025

data.title: BofA raises Zion Bancorp's PT citing good growth prospects and strong Q3 results; upgrades rating
data.description:
** BofA raises regional lender Zions Bancorp's PT to $62 from $59; upgrades to "neutral" from "underperform"

** Brokerage believes the company's current price to earnings ratio, which is about 19% lower vs historical average, does not account for bank's potential for growth

** Also notes the company reported a rise in third-quarter profit this week, boosted by increased interest income and broader loan growth amid easing economic uncertainty

** Net interest income, the difference between what banks earn on loans and pay on deposits, rose to $672 mln from $620 mln in the year-ago quarter

** Seven of 25 brokerages rate the stock "buy", 17 "hold" and one "sell"; median PT $62 — LSEG

** Including session's moves, stock down ~3.9% YTD

data.title: Union Pacific Defends Mega Railroad Merger After Rival's Criticisms — WSJ
data.description:
By Esther Fung

Union Pacific defended its proposed $71.5 billion railroad merger after a rival said the merger is "unnecessary, anti-competitive and bad" for the U.S. economy.

BNSF Railway, which Union Pacific competes with in the western half of the country, said that the proposed merger between Union Pacific and Norfolk Southern will "concentrate too much control of the nation's freight market with one railroad leading to long-term harm to competition and resilience in the nation's supply chain."

The Berkshire Hathaway-owned railroad said the merger will result in less competition and higher shipping rates for customers. BNSF added that 300 intermodal lanes could be eliminated if the merger is approved.

"When the railroads come up and say very misleading positions, it helps us," said Union Pacific's chief executive Jim Vena in the company's earnings call Thursday. "They (the regulators) know we're not going to remove 300 lanes of traffic, okay."

For customers who will see their railroad options reduced from two to one, Vena said, "we are going to provide access to those locations to another railroad to give them the optionality that they had before."

Many shippers, especially chemical companies, remain skeptical about the merger. They said that their experience with past mergers resulted in worse service and higher shipping rates.

Union Pacific plans to submit its formal merger application to regulators as soon as November.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Western Alliance Bancorp Is Maintained at Outperform by Keefe, Bruyette & Woods
W.Africa Crude-Differentials steady as traders mull Russia sanctions impact
data.description:
West African crude oil differentials were little changed on Thursday, as traders weighed the possible knock-on effects of fresh U.S. and EU sanctions on Russia on other crude markets.

* Both buyers and sellers are in wait and see mode, one trader said on Thursday.

* U.S. President Donald Trump hit Russia's two biggest oil companies with sanctions in a sharp policy shift on Moscow's war in Ukraine, prompting global oil prices to rise by 5% on Thursday and India to consider cutting Russian imports.

* Stronger freight rates and a return to backwardation on the Brent forward curve have also impacted arbitrage economics for shipments to Asia, the trader added.

* The West African market was already quiet this week as traders awaited term allocations, according to one trade source.

* No offers have been made for December yet, a trader said on Thursday, while a small overhang of November cargoes also remains.

* Tenders coming up include Hindustan Petroleum Corp Ltd for January and Pertamina for early December, but neither company is expected to buy much West African crude in those, the source said.

data.title: Mexico's Alsea slides on Q3 miss, guidance cut
data.description:
** Shares in Mexican restaurant and cafe chain operator Alsea slide over 3% on Thursday, after it trims its 2025 guidance and Q3 earnings land below analysts forecasts

** Firm trims 2025 revenue forecast to high single-digit growth versus a prior low-double-digit estimate, and its core earnings forecast to a low single digit growth from a prior mid single-digit, says JPMorgan citing earning call

** Alsea points to worse-than-expected demand in September, as Starbucks continues to struggle in France and Mexican customers favor lower-priced items, JPMorgan says

** For Q3, Alsea reported a net profit up at 367 million pesos ($20 million), below analysts' 568 million forecast, while revenues hit 21.15 billion pesos versus an LSEG poll estimate of 22.16 billion

** Company predicts 6 billion pesos in capex this year and 5-5.5 billion pesos capex in 2026 as it focuses on remodeling stores

** Analysts at Scotiabank cite a "mixed bag" with positive signals from some countries, while those at Santander are optimistic: "We believe margins will improve in the coming quarters."

** Alsea's stock recovers ground in late morning after losing over 5% in early trading

($1 = 18.3694 Mexican pesos)

data.title: US to probe China’s compliance with 2020 trade deal, New York Times reports
data.description:
The U.S. is preparing to investigate China's adherence to the terms of a trade deal signed during President Donald Trump's first term, the New York Times reported on Thursday citing a person familiar.

The Section 301 probe could be announced as early as Friday, the Times reported, and could result in additional tariffs between the U.S. and China during a period of heightened trade tensions before a meeting next week between Trump and China's Xi Jinping.

Trade tensions between the U.S. and China, the world's two biggest economies, flared in recent weeks after months of relative calm. Trump imposed additional duties of 100% on China that are due to take effect on November 1 after China announced export controls on nearly all rare earths.

data.title: GE Vernova Stock Bounces Back. Here's What Helping It. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

The artificial-intelligence trade isn't dead after all.

GE Vernova stock was back in the green Thursday with some help from Wall Street.

The stock had closed lower the previous session, despite a better-than-expected earnings report. Wednesday's wild stock swings seemed to stem from investors' concerns that things were as good as they were going to get for the stock. Shares traded as high as $609.54 and as low as $532.72 before closing the session at $576, down 1.6%.

Coming into the earnings report, GE Vernova stock was up about 80% year to date, boosted by the buildout of power-hungry AI data centers, which is driving demand for GE Vernova products.

But GE Vernova stock rose 2.5% to $590.54 Thursday, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average were up 0.4% and 0.1%, respectively.

Helping restore confidence in GE Vernova on Thursday was an upgrade from CFRA analyst Jonathan Sakraida. He took his rating to Buy from Hold and raised the target price by $10 to $700 a share.

Third-quarter "results were soundly positive, both on the demand...and margin fronts...indicating that AI infrastructure spending remains robust," wrote Sakraida. "Our base assumption is that GE Vernova meets or exceeds its 2026 to 2027 target[s]."

GE Vernova projects it can grow sales at a mid-single digit rate, in percent terms, over the coming years and produce earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, or Ebitda, profit margins of 14%. Wall Street expects 2025 Ebitda margins of about 9%.

"The Power/AI/Momentum group unwound for no apparent reason," said TD Cowen analyst Marc Bianchi in a report following earnings. "The company's fundamental update was incrementally positive with strong Power and Electrification orders and solid margin performance."

He rates shares Buy and has a $680 price target for the stock.

Overall, about 62% of analysts covering GE Vernova stock rate shares Buy, according to FactSet. The average Buy-rating ratio for stocks in the S&P 500 is about 55%.

The average analyst price target for GE Vernova stock is about $$682 a share, up about $2 since the company reported earnings.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Enphase Will Struggle In Changing Residential Solar Market — Market Talk
data.description:
1320 ET - Enphase Energy may have a difficult time adjusting to the new solar landscape with a tax credit for homeowner-owned solar systems set to expire at the end of the year, say Mizuho analysts, who downgrade the stock to neutral. Residential solar demand will fall 20% in 2026 and the market will transition more toward third-party owned systems. Enphase is trying to roll out its own alternative to homeowner owned systems, launching a hybrid lease product that would be less expensive than a traditional third-party owned system. But such a product will likely be too complex for homeowners, the analysts say.(nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1234 ET - More of American Airlines' premium customers seem to be flying for pleasure rather than business. Within American's premium cabins, 65% of the load factor appears to be leisure travelers rather than corporate travelers, Strategy Chief Steve Johnson says on a call with analysts. "What we're seeing is a renaissance or maybe a new beginning for premium leisure," he says. Premium leisure flyers bring stronger yields, though premium business still has yields that are 1.5 to 2 times higher than what American gets from other sources, CFO Devon May says. "We'd love to fill the airplane with business travelers and premium leisure travelers," May says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1228 ET - American Airlines is investing in enhancing the premium travel experience, CEO Robert Isom says on a call with analysts. The carrier is installing high-speed satellite Wi-Fi on its regional aircraft and now offers gate-to-gate satellite Wi-Fi on more planes than any other airline, with complementary access coming to loyalty members in January, the CEO says. American is also offering new amenity kits and improved food and beverage options, aided by a new partnership with Champagne Bellanger, he says. It has also entered into a new coffee partnership with Lavazza, the CEO says. "We continue to explore partnerships to elevate the art of travel," he says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1223 ET - CEO Robert Isom tells analysts that American Airlines is a "premium global airline" as it looks to stake its claim in the thriving market for premium airfare. Carriers are fighting to lure more high-earning passengers who continue to travel and spend while growth lags for basic economy seats. Premium revenue outperformed main-cabin revenue at American in 3Q, according to its CFO. The airline is looking to grow its count of premium seats at nearly two times the rate of main-cabin seats, Isom says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1220 ET - American Airlines is aggressively using its loyalty program to win back customers, especially in competitive markets, CEO Robert Isom says on a call with analysts. Active AAdvantage accounts rose 7% during 3Q, he says. The biggest growth came from enrollments out of Chicago--where the carrier faces strong competition from United Airlines at O'Hare International--with account growth rising 20% there, the CEO says. Loyalty members are more engaged and generate a higher yield compared within nonmembers, he says. They are also a key driver of premium cabin demand, which U.S. airlines are now leaning on as main-cabin demand lags. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1217 ET - T-Mobile expects a competitive edge from its partnership with SpaceX's satellite business, incoming CEO Srini Gopalan says during an appearance on CNBC. The partnership, which allows users to send text messages and access some apps in areas cellphone signals can't reach, should put T-Mobile two to three years ahead of its competitors in the space, Gopalan says. "We've pretty much, with Space X, invented the category," he says. "We love the fact that they are doubling down on the category." (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1213 ET - Apple's new iPhone 17 runs fastest on T-Mobile's network, a key driver for the cell carrier's 3Q results, incoming CEO Srini Gopalan says during an appearance on CNBC. The new model runs 90% faster on T-Mobile's network than that of one of its benchmark competitors and 40% faster than the other competitor, Gopalan says. "That's why you're seeing people flocking to T-Mobile with the new iPhone," he says. "The iPhone's been a big driver to our latest quarter."(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1208 ET - T-Mobile has an increasingly unique offering compared to the rest of the industry, incoming CEO Srini Gopalan says during an appearance on CNBC. "Our differentiation is just widening in this industry, and that converted into outstanding financials," Gopalan says, noting the company notched record new customer additions in 3Q. He says no other player in the industry has tried to differentiate itself. When asked how T-Mobile is able to offer so many deals, Gopalan says the company believes it is rewarded with customer loyalty. "If you do the right things for customers, they will reward you," he says.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1154 ET - Rising oil prices due to U.S. sanctions on Russia are unlikely to boost inflation, Franklin Templeton's Sonal Desai says. "If you are talking about 4%, 5%, 6% increases in the price of oil, I actually don't see that as having a huge impact on inflation," as services carry a much heavier weight on consumer price gauges, Desai says. A sustained increase in energy costs could end up boosting prices across the economy, but flip-flopping Trump policies make it hard to predict, she says. September CPI is due tomorrow and Desai expects a slight acceleration. WTI crude rises 5%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1147 ET - Tesla reported mixed 3Q results, but that's not particularly important for the stock value, which is mostly fueled by the potential of unproven AI projects with a wide range of outcomes, Truist analysts say. "The vast majority of near-term earnings is coming primarily from autos, and secondarily from the energy business. Yet, we estimate only 11% of TSLA's valuation is related to autos and another 11% from the energy business," the analysts say. The rest is related to the company's long-term AI bets, including self-driving cars, robotaxis and humanoid robots, which "are at once quite unproven (close to zero revenue), and potentially extremely valuable." (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1118 ET - The oil price rally as the U.S. tightens sanctions on Russia provides a window for producers to hedge their production before the looming supply glut and the administration's push for lower oil prices ahead of 2026 mid-term elections, Citi's Francesco Martoccia says in a note. While the sharpest moves are at the front of the curve, WTI futures for 2026 have moved back above $60 a barrel, he writes. But the hedging window may close quickly. "We believe the geopolitical premium is set to erode, because the enforcement gaps could widen and the supply chain of shadow fleets and middlemen adapts, and especially as the loosening oil market fundamentals become more evident in the data." WTI is up 4.8% at $61.30 a barrel and Brent is 4.6% higher at $65.46 a barrel.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1110 ET - IBM is seeing increased momentum in its GenAI segment. Wedbush says the company's GenAI book of business continues to accelerate with its backlog now reaching $9.5 billion, well above the prior period of $7.5 billion as the company is seeing increasing demand for its AI agents and assistants while expanding its reach across various verticals. This complements 3Q results, which Wedbush says featured beats across the board while slightly raising full year revenue and free cash flow guidance "as the company gains further momentum across its entire portfolio while its GenAI book of business continues to gain traction." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: Trump Sanctions Aim at Russian Oil, but the Real Target Might Be Elsewhere — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Martin Baccardax

Wayne Gretzky, perhaps the greatest ice hockey player of all time, says much of his success was based on advice from his father, who told him to "skate to where the puck is going, not where it's been."

Investors looking for insight on U.S. trade talks with China would be well served to focus on where the negotiations are headed, as opposed to how markets might be interpreting them right now.

President Donald Trump's decision to impose sanctions on two Russian oil producers, while floating the idea of curbs on U.S. software exports and tariffs that would amount to an embargo on trade, suggests the puck is heading somewhere he isn't happy about. We could be seeing an effort to change its direction.

The blacklisting of the Russian oil companies Rosneft and Lukoil, which account for around half of the country's exports, is ostensibly tied to the president's efforts to broker a peace agreement in Russia's war with Ukraine.

The larger impact, however, is likely to be felt in China, which relies on Russia for around a fifth of its overall crude imports. "The key question is whether these sanctions are enough to deter buyers of Russian oil, specifically China and India," said Warren Patterson, head of commodities strategy at ING.

Choking off supplies as Beijing looks to meet its target for economic growth over the final months of the year could prove crucial in establishing U.S. leverage in the on-again/off-again negotiations between Washington and Beijing, although it could also amount to a provocation that would hinder efforts to reduce tensions on trade.

The U.S. needs leverage because China controls most of the world's production of rare-earth minerals, which are crucial to the U.S. tech industry. While President Donald Trump is moving quickly to secure access to rare earths — he signed on to an $8.5 billion partnership with Australia this week, and the government is investing in rare-earths mining — those efforts won't bear fruit quickly.

And the clock is ticking toward the end of the month, when Trump may meet with Chinese President Xi Jinping at the APEC summit in South Korea. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, along with U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer, is slated to meet with China Vice Premier Le Lifeng in Malaysia on Friday.

Late Wednesday, Bessent told reporters in Washington that "everything is on the table" in the current round of talks, but insisted that broader export controls on things like software or aircraft engines "will likely be in coordination with our G-7 allies."

That suggestion is another hint that talks aren't moving in the direction that Trump would like, given his long dislike for multilateral negotiations and his insistence that his relationship with Xi would be the key catalyst in reaching an agreement.

Seema Shah, chief global strategist at Principal Asset Management, thinks the path toward any U.S.-China deal will be long and bumpy. Trade friction is likely to become part of the economic landscape, she argues.

"Further escalation remains possible, and even post-resolution, tariffs and non-tariff measures (such as export controls and investment restrictions) are poised to remain a key geopolitical lever, especially as the U.S. and China continue their strategic rivalry in AI and technology," she said.

China, meanwhile, has been subtly reworking its global export engine through a sharp devaluation of its yuan against the euro, which has helped it offset the impact of tariffs on U.S.-bound goods.

"Global trade flows are proving highly adaptable to U.S. tariffs, bending and twisting to minimize their impact, both on U.S. inflation and Chinese exports," said Julian Evans-Pritchard, head of China economics at Capital Economics.

But he warned that investors would be "unwise to assume that China has developed lasting immunity" to levies from Washington, saying a trade- war escalation could have a deeper and broader effect on China's economy than the U.S.'s existing tariffs and export restrictions.

The same might be said for the U.S., where investments in artificial intelligence have "kept the economy out of a recession," according to James Egelhof, chief U.S. economist at BNP Paribas. Beijing restricting access to key Chinese markets, or closing the taps on rare-earth elements, would reverse that rather swiftly.

The trade-war puck is heading toward an unfamiliar corner of the global arena, and one in which China has developed a surprising edge. Trump, who earlier this week suggested that tariffs were "more powerful" than China's rare-earth dominance, might be skating in the wrong direction.

Write to Martin Baccardax at martin.baccardax@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: So much for 'Uptober.' Crypto markets will be recovering from this month's historic $20 billion wipeout for a long time.
data.description:
By Frances Yue

Smaller cryptocurrencies are struggling to find their footing after a $20 billion wipeout

The market for altcoins has continued to struggle following the biggest liquidation event in crypto's history.

Heading into October, crypto investors were eager to see more gains. "Uptober," they were calling it.

But instead, the month has mostly been marked by losses following the biggest single-day liquidation event in the history of cryptocurrency, which wiped out at least $20 billion in positions. Almost two weeks later, the digital-asset market is still reeling.

See: Bitcoin rebounded after the biggest crypto liquidation in history. But a choppy road lies ahead for investors.

While bitcoin's (BTCUSD) price has stabilized, smaller coins are struggling to find their footing. The market capitalization of cryptocurrencies excluding bitcoin has fallen by over 18% since Oct. 10, according to data from TradingView.

The value of some smaller cryptocurrencies fell up to 90% in minutes during the crash earlier this month. A few tokens, such as Cosmos (ATOMUSD), saw their value nearly wiped out on Binance on Oct. 10, before bouncing back. Cosmo was trading at around $3.21 on Thursday, down 20% over the last month, and off 93% from its all-time high of $44.70 reached on Sept. 19, 2021, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

The liquidation event has continued to weigh on sentiment and may have a lasting impact, said Alex Thorn, head of firmwide research at Galaxy Digital. "If a broad altcoin rally is still in the cards, it will likely take weeks or months or longer to materialize."

Altcoins hit the hardest

The selloff on Oct. 10 was triggered by President Donald Trump's threatening of an additional 100% tariff against China, effective Nov. 1. But the liquidation was amplified by the limited liquidity of many cryptocurrencies, as well as infrastructure issues on some digital asset exchanges, said Martin Leinweber, director of digital asset research & strategy at benchmark administrator MarketVector Indexes.

Altcoins were hit much harder than bitcoin because of their thin liquidity, Leinweber said in a phone interview. Altcoins refer to cryptocurrencies other than bitcoin.

For now, only 16.5% of altcoins are currently outperforming bitcoin, Leinweber added.

Bitcoin has outperformed altcoins for over two years, noted Leinweber.

"We've always suspected that the altcoin market is driven by just a few market makers, versus in bitcoin, there are so many participants that are market making and providing liquidity in different ways," Shubh Varma, co-founder and chief executive at Hyblock Capital, said in an interview. Market makers are financial firms or individuals that provide liquidity by committing to buy and sell securities.

"There's always been this sort of suspicion that if these two, three market makers went away, there wouldn't be a lot of liquidity [for some altcoins]. And I think we kind of saw that being true," Varma said.

When looking at the order books for crypto, or a live list of all the buy and sell orders on Oct. 10, there was a drastic reduction in the liquidity once crypto prices started falling, Shubh said. As a result, even small trades could drive prices lower, he added.

Could altcoins recover?

It may take a while for altcoins to recover, Galaxy's Thorn said.

During the Oct. 10 selloff, a cohort of traders considered to be "sophisticated retail investors" was hurt the most, Thorn said in emailed comments shared with MarketWatch. This group was mostly made up of relatively wealthy crypto traders with blockchain experience and had the most enduring interest in altcoins, Thorn said.

These traders will "need to regroup and stabilize further before a material broad rally in altcoins is possible," he added.

Still, MarketVector Indexes' Leinweber said he is optimistic that the broader crypto market, including altcoins, could rebound, given the favorable macroeconomic environment. The Federal Reserve is widely expected to continue to cut its key interest rate, while the U.S. economy has remained resilient so far, he said.

"In general, most risk assets are performing well and the rate is expected to fall," Leinweber said. "Everything is on a green light here, but not crypto." As cryptocurrencies still trade mostly as risk assets, Leinweber said he expected them to eventually catch up.

Bitcoin (BTCUSD) rose 2.4% on Thursday to around $110,225, according to FactSet data. U.S. stocks traded higher on Thursday, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA up 0.3%. The S&P 500 SPX traded roughly 0.6% higher and the Nasdaq composite COMP rose 0.9%.

-Frances Yue

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Ribbon Communications Shares Sink on Downbeat 4Q Outlook
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Shares of Ribbon Communications fell after the company issued a downbeat outlook for the current quarter, after narrowing its loss and notching slightly higher revenue in the third quarter.

The stock sank 16% on Thursday, to $3.36. Shares are down 15% since the beginning of the year.

The company, which supplies communications technology and IP optical networking solutions, late Wednesday posted a third-quarter loss of $12.1 million, or 7 cents a share, compared with a loss of $13.4 million, or 8 cents a share, a year earlier.

Stripping out certain one-time items, adjusted earnings came in at 4 cents a share. Analysts surveyed by FactSet had expected adjusted earnings of 6 cents a share.

Revenue rose 2.4% to $215.4 million but missed the $220.1 million that Wall Street modeled.

Chief Executive Bruce McClelland said the federal government shutdown had a minor impact on the company's cloud & edge unit.

For the current quarter, Ribbon guided for adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization, which strip out exceptional and other one-off items, of $42 million to $48 million on revenue of $230 million to $250 million. Analysts were looking for adjusted Ebitda of $59.5 million on revenue of $255 million.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: DesignRush Reveals Top Branding Agencies for October 2025 as Trusted Brands See 51% Higher Spend
data.description:
DesignRush has announced the top branding agencies for October 2025, recognizing firms that help businesses build trusted brands.

New York, New York--(Newsfile Corp. - October 23, 2025) - Leading B2B platform DesignRush has announced its list of top branding agencies for October 2025.

The recognition underscores the growing emphasis on memorable branding as a key driver of success.

A 2024 study by Forter found that consumers spend 51% more with brands they trust, while research by Edelman shows that 81% of consumers require brand trust before making a purchase.

"In both B2C and B2B, trust compresses skepticism and builds loyalty," said Amore Watters, Global Marketing Manager at DesignRush.

"A strong brand does not just look good; it signals reliability, consistency, and credibility. That is what drives long-term growth. In B2B, brand trust is crucial for fostering lasting relationships and securing repeat business. A recognizable and consistent brand can shorten sales cycles and support long-term engagement."

To help businesses build brands that stand out and inspire trust, DesignRush has released its October 2025 list of top branding agencies, recognized for developing strong visual and strategic identities that connect with audiences.

These agencies specialize in color, typography, and design systems that create cohesive and memorable brands.

The top branding agencies for October 2025 are:

1. Hinge Marketing

  • Location: Virginia, United States
  • Industries: Professional Services, AEC, Financial Services, Consulting, Technology
  • Website: hingemarketing.com
  • Project Highlight: Thiel & Team, a Texas-based architecture, interior design, and procurement firm specializing in high-end hospitality and multifamily projects, completed a full rebrand and new website in partnership with Hinge. The project included updated brand positioning, a complete visual refresh, and a high-performance website that showcased the firm's expertise and strengthened its market presence. The rebrand earned Hermes, Communicator, and SMPS MCA Awards. Within three months, the website achieved a 50% increase in traffic and a 300% boost in digital visibility.

2. Crescita Solutions

Brands can explore the top branding agencies by location, size, average hourly rate, and portfolio - all on DesignRush.

data.title: Valero Energy Shares Gain on Strong 3Q Results
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Valero Energy shares rose after the company's third-quarter results beat analyst expectations.

The stock was up 6% to $171.71 Thursday. Shares have gained 40% this year.

The petroleum refiner reported $32.17 billion in revenue during the quarter, ahead of the $29.97 billion Wall Street was projecting, according to FactSet.

Adjusted earnings per share were $3.66, also beating analysts' forecast of $3.05.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Super Micro's latest financial update adds to credibility concerns
data.description:
By Emily Bary

The company expects to miss its quarterly revenue target due to order timing. While Super Micro is sticking with its full-year outlook, it has a track record of guidance disappointments.

Super Micro's stock was one of the S&P 500's biggest decliners on Thursday.

Super Micro Computer Inc. expects to fall short of its prior revenue outlook, in another setback for investors.

The server maker said on Thursday that it estimates September-quarter sales of about $5 billion, whereas the company's prior forecast called for $6 billion to $7 billion. Super Micro will officially post earnings on Nov. 4.

Super Micro said the anticipated shortfall relates to "design win upgrades" that will shift some revenue into the December quarter but had previously been expected during the September quarter.

"We see customer demand accelerating, and we are gaining AI share," Chief Executive Charles Liang said in a release. The company maintained its forecast for at least $33 billion in revenue for its June-ending fiscal year, "with the expectation of delivering more."

Super Micro shares (SMCI) were down more than 7% in afternoon action Thursday to rank as the second-worst performer in the S&P 500 SPX on the day.

Raymond James analyst Simon Leopold said the announcement "does not help build investor confidence," but he thought the issue was "largely timing related and not thesis altering."

See more: A lot has changed for Intel in three months. Earnings are the next big test.

He has an outperform rating on the stock but noted that there seem to be "credibility issues" with Super Micro. For instance, Leopold's fiscal 2026 forecast calls for $31.9 billion in revenue, the consensus view is for $32.1 billion, and two-thirds of the estimates included in the FactSet analyst consensus are below management's $33 billion outlook.

"We consider our conservatism justified, but this keeps us hopeful that [Super Micro] can exceed expectations," Leopold wrote.

There have been disappointments in the past - including when Super Micro cut its fiscal 2025 outlook multiple times and when the company had to revise down its initial expectations for fiscal 2026, which once called for $40 billion.

Mizuho analyst Vijay Rakesh wrote Thursday that despite "strong design wins" for Super Micro, he thinks Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL) is winning market share among tier-2 cloud providers and enterprise customers "while seeing advantages with financing."

Dell's stock was up more than 3% on Thursday, at last check.

Read: Broadcom left investors with a mystery. This analyst thinks he's cracked the case.

-Emily Bary

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: C&F Financial 3Q Interest Income $38.8M
Euronext wheat steady as traders assess exports
data.description:
Euronext wheat ended little changed on Thursday as traders assessed heightened tensions over the war in Ukraine, rumours of Chinese interest in foreign wheat and results from an Algerian tender that underscored stiff export competition.

December wheat (BL2Z5) on the Paris-based Euronext market settled flat on the day at 189.75 euros ($221.29) a metric ton. Deferred positions closed slightly higher.

Results from an Algerian import tender, in which traders said the North African country's state buyer bought around 600,000 tons, suggested merchants would choose supplies from the Black Sea region and possibly Argentina, with France thought to be tacitly excluded again due to a diplomatic rift between Paris and Algiers.

“This is bearish as such big export markets cannot easily be replaced by France at this time of big supplies in the big exporting regions. We need China back as a buyer,” one German trader said.

China has reduced its wheat imports and not taken any French wheat for over a year. However, traders said there was market talk that China was asking for price offers of wheat from the European Union and Argentina, adding this may be simply price-checking as no deals were reported.

Argentine wheat is currently the cheapest export origin worldwide at around $204-$209 a ton free on board (FOB).

The looming harvest there is expected to produce a bumper volume but traders said it was too early to anticipate crop quality. The November South American shipment period specified in Algeria’s tender may also be too early for merchants to secure Argentine supplies for that order.

Chicago wheat rose more sharply to move away from a five-year low struck last week. The gains were supported by investor hopes of progress in U.S. trade talks with China and reaction to U.S. sanctions on two major Russian oil firms, the first Ukraine-related sanctions on Russia to be issued by U.S. President Donald Trump in his second term.

An increase in the International Grains Council's monthly forecast for 2025/26 global wheat production to a new record nonetheless highlighted ample supply in exporting countries.

($1 = 0.8575 euros)
